claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 12 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 4 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start the assessment, I need to ask you some questions about your child or children.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility depends on age:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age. If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can check each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this! I have three children actually - Alex is 7, Blake is 12, and Charlie is 10. I'd like to know if I can claim for all of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children:
- **Alex** (age 7)
- **Blake** (age 12)
- **Charlie** (age 10)

All three of your children are under 16, which is great - they fall into the age category that generally qualifies for Child Benefit. Now I need to ask you a few more questions to complete the eligibility assessment.

**Next question: Are any of your children working full-time (16 hours or more per week)?**

This applies to Alex (7), Blake (12), and Charlie (10). Please let me know if any of them are working 16 hours or more per week, or if none of them are working full-time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them work at all - they're all in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since none of your children are working full-time, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are any of your children receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

For example, Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own name (not as part of your household claim)?

Given their ages (7, 10, and 12), this would be quite unusual, but I need to confirm that none of them are receiving benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're just regular kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are any of your children in an apprenticeship?**

Given that Alex (7), Blake (12), and Charlie (10) are all in primary school, I assume none of them are in apprenticeships. Can you confirm that's correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, none of them are in apprenticeships - they're all too young for that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me move on to questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, or
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold), or
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days), or
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family, or
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources, or
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national, or
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK, or does any of the other circumstances apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK actually. I'm currently living abroad and none of those other circumstances apply to me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since you're living abroad and none of the special circumstances apply, I need to ask:

**Are you a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces serving abroad), or are you living in a country that has a reciprocal agreement with the UK for Child Benefit?**

Some people living abroad can still claim Child Benefit if they're:
- Working as a Crown servant abroad, or
- Living in certain qualifying countries (such as some EEA countries or countries with specific agreements)

Could you tell me:
1. Why you're living abroad (work-related, personal reasons, etc.)?
2. Which country you're currently living in?

This will help me determine if you might still qualify under the residency rules.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living abroad for personal reasons, not work-related. I'd rather not say exactly which country, but it's not one with any special agreement with the UK - just a regular situation where I've moved abroad with my family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. I understand your situation now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Result**

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for any of your three children: Alex, Blake, or Charlie.

### **Why you're not eligible:**

While your children meet all the requirements (they're all under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in apprenticeships), **you do not meet the UK residency requirements** that are necessary to claim Child Benefit.

To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Meet one of the special circumstances (such as being a Crown servant abroad, having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or living in a country with a reciprocal agreement)

Since you're living abroad for personal reasons and none of the special circumstances apply to you, you unfortunately don't qualify.

### **What this means:**

Child Benefit is specifically designed for people who are resident in the UK or who have specific qualifying connections to the UK. Living abroad without one of the qualifying circumstances means you cannot claim this benefit.

### **If your situation changes:**

If you return to live in the UK in the future, or if your circumstances change (for example, if you become a Crown servant), you would be able to apply for Child Benefit at that time.

For more information, you can visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility

I understand this may be disappointing news. Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about this decision?

reasonings: Alex (age 7) meets the age requirement as they are under 16, is not working full-time, is not receiving benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad for personal reasons and do not meet any of the special circumstances (such as being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status, or living in a qualifying country). Therefore, the claim for Alex is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.

Blake (age 12) meets the age requirement as they are under 16, is not working full-time, is not receiving benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad for personal reasons and do not meet any of the special circumstances (such as being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status, or living in a qualifying country). Therefore, the claim for Blake is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.

Charlie (age 10) meets the age requirement as they are under 16, is not working full-time, is not receiving benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad for personal reasons and do not meet any of the special circumstances (such as being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status, or living in a qualifying country). Therefore, the claim for Charlie is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children (Alex age 7, Blake age 12, and Charlie age 10) who all meet the child qualification requirements - they are all under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in apprenticeships. However, the claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and does not meet any of the UK residency requirements necessary to claim Child Benefit. The claimant does not normally live in the UK, is not a Crown servant abroad, does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and is not living in a qualifying country with a reciprocal agreement. Therefore, the claim is ineligible for all three children due to the claimant's failure to meet the residency requirements.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
